behaviors.description: Scoring model assigns correct risk score when transaction exceeds configured amount threshold for its merchant category
behaviors.justification: The behavior depends on configurable rules that must be loaded and applied. A unit test with a mocked config object would miss real failures: config file parsing errors, type conversion bugs (e.g., string vs. numeric comparison), or the threshold not being read from the actual config source. You need integration-level testing with actual configuration loading and a real transaction object to ensure the threshold comparison works end-to-end.
behaviors.test_description: Load configuration from the actual config source (file/database), create a transaction with an amount just above and just below the threshold, run the scoring model, verify the risk score differs between the two cases. Repeat for multiple merchant categories to ensure category-specific thresholds are applied.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Integration is sufficient for the behavior scope. System-level testing (deployment, external config service) is not needed unless configuration is loaded from an external service. The failure mode — wrong threshold application — is caught by integration testing.

behaviors.description: Risk model identifies fraudulent transaction pattern (rapid small purchases, different geographies) even when no single rule threshold is exceeded
behaviors.justification: This behavior requires the risk model to actually perform inference on pattern data. Mocking the model's output would completely sidestep the assertion you're trying to test — that the model can detect the pattern. You need the real model artifact (pre-trained weights, if ML-based) running on test data that reproduces the pattern. A unit test with a mocked model will always pass, hiding the actual failure: the model doesn't learn this pattern.
behaviors.test_description: Craft a test transaction sequence: 3+ small purchases (<$50 each) from different geographic locations within a short time window, no single transaction exceeding typical thresholds. Load the actual risk model, pass the transaction context (merchant category, user purchase history, device fingerprint) through the model, verify fraud probability exceeds a threshold (e.g., >0.7). Also test a non-fraudulent sequence (same geographies, normal timing) returns low probability.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: If the risk model is non-deterministic (e.g., uses dropout, sampling, or calls an external LLM-based API), this should be Agentic instead. Integration assumes the model is deterministic on fixed inputs. Confirm whether the risk model is a local, deterministic ML model or involves external/non-deterministic components.

behaviors.description: Velocity check in scoring model correctly counts transactions within configured time window per user
behaviors.justification: Counting transactions requires querying transaction history from a data store. A unit test with a mocked database would miss: query filtering bugs (e.g., missing the user_id filter), off-by-one errors in window boundaries, timezone handling in timestamp comparisons, or deduplication logic in the query layer. The real failure surface is in the database layer — incorrect query, wrong index, or data inconsistency. Integration testing with a real (test) database is the minimum to catch these.
behaviors.test_description: Set up a test database with 10+ transactions for a specific user, with known timestamps. Insert transactions within and outside the configured time window (e.g., 10 minutes). Call the velocity check for that user with the window config, verify it returns the exact count of transactions within the window. Test boundary cases: transaction at exactly the window start/end, overlapping windows, timezone transitions if applicable.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Integration is appropriate. System-level testing (distributed clock synchronization, real-world database load) would be overkill for testing counting logic. The failure mode — wrong count — is caught by integration testing with a test database.

behaviors.description: Transaction flagged by risk model but not by scoring model is still held for review — models operate as OR gate, not AND
behaviors.justification: The behavior is not just about the OR logic (which a unit test could verify), but about the transaction being "held for review" — an end-to-end outcome. The failure mode is that the transaction passes through unexamined despite being flagged. A unit test mocking both models and testing the Boolean condition would miss failures in the downstream mechanism: the holding queue/database not receiving the transaction, the review flag not persisting, or the review system rejecting the format. You need integration testing to verify the transaction actually enters the holding pipeline.
behaviors.test_description: Create a test transaction. Mock the scoring model to return flag=false, mock the risk model to return flag=true. Pass to the fraud detection service, verify the combined result triggers a hold. Check that the transaction is actually inserted into the holding queue/database and marked for review. Reverse the mock flags (scoring=true, risk=false) and verify holding is still triggered.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Integration captures the end-to-end behavior. System-level testing would be needed only if the holding mechanism uses an external queue (e.g., Kafka, SQS) or distributed consensus. Assuming the holding mechanism is local (in-process queue or test database), integration is sufficient.

behaviors.description: Combined output includes both numeric risk score and risk model's fraud probability so reviewer can see which model flagged the transaction and why
behaviors.justification: The behavior is about the output structure: it must include two specific fields. A unit test with mocked models is sufficient to verify the combined output object has both fields with correct values. The failure mode — a missing or incorrect field — is caught without needing real models or downstream systems. Mocking the scoring and risk models at the unit level allows you to test the composition logic in isolation.
behaviors.test_description: Mock the scoring model to return {score: 75}. Mock the risk model to return {fraud_probability: 0.85}. Call the service's output-combining method, verify the result contains both fields with exact values. Test edge cases: score=0, probability=1.0, probability=0. Verify field names match the schema expected by the review system.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Unit is appropriate for the stated behavior (output includes both fields). However, if the review system parses or validates this output format, an Integration test would catch parsing/validation failures. But the behavior statement is about the service including both fields, not about downstream consumption, so Unit suffices.
